skill.name: css-animations
skill.description: Guidance for building deterministic CSS keyframe animations that HyperFrames can preview, seek, and render consistently.
skill.summary: Build seekable CSS keyframe animations for HyperFrames previews and renders.
skill.author: heygen-com

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command-execution and blocker patterns, but review found they are caused mainly by Markdown inline code, CSS examples, and normal documentation text. The only real executable commands are hardcoded HyperFrames validation commands, and the only network indicators are MDN documentation links. No prompt injection, credential access, data exfiltration, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Documented validation commands require normal command execution
low_findings.description: The skill documents hardcoded commands for HyperFrames linting and validation. These commands are not dynamically constructed and do not include user input, but running npx can execute package tooling in the user's project environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fenced bash block contains direct npx commands. The risk is low because the commands are fixed validation steps and there is no injection vector in the skill text.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded external documentation links
low_findings.description: The skill links to MDN CSS animation documentation. These are normal reference links and do not send local data or credentials, but they are external network destinations if opened.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible MDN documentation links. No evidence found of network calls, tracking parameters, or data exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Static command and blocker detections are false positives
low_findings.description: Most static alerts map to Markdown inline code, fenced HTML and CSS examples, and descriptive animation terms. No Ruby backtick execution, weak cryptography, or system reconnaissance behavior is present in the reviewed skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the cited lines shows documentation prose and CSS or HTML examples. No executable Ruby syntax, cryptographic operation, or host reconnaissance command appears there.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded npx validation commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The only confirmed command pattern is a documentation example that asks users to run HyperFrames linting and validation with npx.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are present in a bash code block, but they are hardcoded and limited to expected validation tooling.

content.value_statement: CSS animation timing can drift when previews and renders need frame-accurate seeking. This skill provides HyperFrames patterns for keyframes, finite durations, delays, and fill modes.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use the HyperFrames CSS runtime adapter.
Shows a seekable keyframe pattern with data-start and data-duration metadata.
Demonstrates staggered CSS animation timing with custom properties.
Recommends finite durations, finite iteration counts, and fill-mode behavior.
Lists validation commands for checking HyperFrames animation compositions.
limitations: Focused on CSS animations for HyperFrames, not general web animation strategy.
Does not provide a full animation runtime or reusable component library.
Recommends GSAP for complex scene choreography instead of CSS-only timelines.
Requires users to verify browser support for seekable CSS animation handles.

use_cases.title: Build render-safe decorative motion
use_cases.description: Create shimmer, glow, mask, grain, or parallax effects that HyperFrames can seek during preview and rendering.

use_cases.title: Add simple timed entrances
use_cases.description: Use CSS keyframes for one-element entrances when a JavaScript timeline would add unnecessary complexity.

use_cases.title: Review animation implementation choices
use_cases.description: Check whether CSS animation timing, fill modes, and iteration counts are suitable for deterministic HyperFrames output.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic CSS Animation
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner user adding a simple render-safe effect.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the css-animations skill to design a simple HyperFrames CSS keyframe animation for a decorative pulse. Include timing, fill mode, and validation guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Convert Motion to Seekable Timing
prompt_templates.scenario: User has an existing CSS effect that must render deterministically.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this CSS animation idea for HyperFrames. Recommend data-start, data-duration, finite iteration count, animation-delay, and animation-fill-mode settings.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Staggered Element Sequence
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate user creating repeated element motion.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the css-animations skill to plan a staggered dot or card animation with CSS custom properties. Keep the motion seekable and finite.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a HyperFrames Animation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user validating production animation behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit my HyperFrames CSS animation approach for deterministic preview and rendering. Identify timing risks, unsupported triggers, layout animation issues, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: A designer wants a three-second glow effect that can be scrubbed in HyperFrames.
output_examples.output: The response recommends a finite keyframe animation, explicit clip metadata, fill-mode both, and validation with HyperFrames tooling.

output_examples.input: A developer asks whether hover-triggered animation is suitable for a rendered clip.
output_examples.output: The response explains that hover triggers are not deterministic for render-critical motion and suggests a timed CSS animation instead.

output_examples.input: A reviewer wants to simplify a one-element entrance animation.
output_examples.output: The response compares CSS keyframes with a JavaScript timeline and recommends CSS when the motion has fixed timing and limited choreography.

best_practices: Use finite animation durations and iteration counts for predictable seeking.
Set animation-fill-mode to both when states must hold before and after motion.
Keep render-critical motion independent from hover, focus, scroll, and media query triggers.
anti_patterns: Using infinite CSS animations without confirming seekable browser animation handles.
Animating layout properties when transform-based motion would produce the same visual result.
Changing animation classes after runtime startup without deterministic timeline control.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create CSS keyframe animations that HyperFrames can seek during preview and rendering.

faq.question: When should I use CSS animations instead of GSAP?
faq.answer: Use CSS for simple one-element motion, decorative loops, and fixed-duration effects. Use GSAP for complex choreography.

faq.question: Why should animations use finite iteration counts?
faq.answer: Finite counts help HyperFrames calculate local animation time, especially when fallback seeking uses negative animation delays.

faq.question: Can this skill handle hover or scroll animations?
faq.answer: It recommends avoiding hover, focus, scroll, and media-query triggers for render-critical motion.

faq.question: Does this skill generate full application code?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and review guidance for CSS animation behavior inside HyperFrames projects.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
